title: वैराग्यशतकम् (प्रमुख श्लोक)
devanagariText:
॥ श्री वैराग्यशतकम् ॥

॥ भर्तृहरिविरचितम् ॥

भोगा न भुक्ता वयमेव भुक्ताः
तपो न तप्तं वयमेव तप्ताः।
कालो न यातो वयमेव याताः
तृष्णा न जीर्णा वयमेव जीर्णाः॥ १॥

भोगे रोगभयं कुले च्युतिभयं वित्ते नृपालाद्भयं
माने दैन्यभयं बले रिपुभयं रूपे जराया भयम्।
शास्त्रे वादिभयं गुणे खलभयं काये कृतान्ताद्भयं
सर्वं वस्तु भयान्वितं भुवि नृणां वैराग्यमेवाभयम्॥ २॥

तृणं न लघु तृणादपि च याचनामानवः
तृणेन बहु दत्तमित्यपि वदन्ति दातारकाः।
तृणस्य लघुता कुतः परमपूज्य मान्यं तृणं
तृणेन हि समानताम् अधिकतामुपैतु स्वयम्॥ ३॥

दुरारध्याश्चामी तुरगचलचित्ताः क्षितिभुजो
वयं च स्थूलेच्छाः सुमहति फले बद्धमनसः।
जरा देहं मृत्युः हरति दयितं जीवितमिदं
सखे नान्यच्छ्रेयो जगति विदुषोऽन्यत्र तपसः॥ ४॥

मातर्मेदिनि तात मारुत सखे तेजः सुबन्धो जल
भ्रातर्व्योम निबद्ध एष भवतामन्त्यः प्रणामाञ्जलिः।
युष्मत्सङ्गवशोपजातसुकृतस्फारस्फुरन्निर्मल-
ज्ञानापास्तसमस्तमोहमहिमा लीये परब्रह्मणि॥ ५॥

तापैः सन्तप्तो गच्छति किल हिमाद्रिं
दारिद्र्येण सन्तप्तो गच्छति धनिनं जनम्।
मोहेन सन्तप्तो गच्छति विषयान्
सर्वत्र सन्तप्तो गच्छति शिवमेव तु॥ ६॥

यां चिन्तयामि सततं मयि सा विरक्ता
साप्यन्यमिच्छति जनं स जनोऽन्यसक्तः।
अस्मत्कृते च परिशुष्यति काचिदन्या
धिक् तां च तं च मदनं च इमां च मां च॥ ७॥

दिनयामिन्यौ सायं प्रातः शिशिरवसन्तौ पुनरायातः।
कालः क्रीडति गच्छत्यायुस्तद्वि न मुञ्चत्याशावायुः॥ ८॥

आशा नाम नदी मनोरथजला तृष्णातरङ्गाकुला
रागग्राहवती वितर्कविहगा धैर्यद्रुमध्वंसिनी।
मोहावर्तसुदुस्तरातिगहना प्रोत्तुङ्गचिन्तातटी
तस्याः पारगता विशुद्धमनसो नन्दन्ति योगीश्वराः॥ ९॥

जीर्णा एव मनोरथाश्च हृदयं नष्टं च षष्ठीजरा-
दोषेणेदम् अशेषम् ऐव वपुषः स्थैर्यं गतं केशवे।
कामो जीर्णधनुर्युवा मदनतापो देशवैदेशिकः
धन्यानां गिरिकन्दरेषु वसतां ज्योतिः परं ध्रूयते॥ १०॥

॥ इति श्री भर्तृहरिकृत वैराग्यशतकम् (प्रमुख श्लोक) सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Vairagya Shatakam ||

|| Bhartrihari-virachitam ||

Bhoga na bhukta vayam-eva bhuktah
Tapo na taptam vayam-eva taptah |
Kalo na yato vayam-eva yatah
Trishna na jirna vayam-eva jirnah || 1 ||

Bhoge roga-bhayam kule chyuti-bhayam vitte nripalad-bhayam
Mane dainya-bhayam bale ripu-bhayam rupe jaraya bhayam |
Shastre vadi-bhayam gune khala-bhayam kaye kritantad-bhayam
Sarvam vastu bhayuanvitam bhuvi nrinam vairagyam-evabhayam || 2 ||

Trinam na laghu trinad-api cha yachana-manavah
Trinena bahu dattam-ityapi vadanti datarakah |
Trinasya laghuta kutah parama-pujya-manyam trinam
Trinena hi samanatam adhikatam-upaitu svayam || 3 ||

Duraradhyash-chami turaga-chala-chittah kshitibhujo
Vayam cha sthula-ichchhah sumahati phale baddha-manasah |
Jara deham mrityuh harati dayitam jivitam-idam
Sakhe nanyach-chreyo jagati vidusho-anyatra tapasah || 4 ||

Matar-Medini tata Maruta sakhe Tejah subandho Jala
Bhratar-Vyoma nibaddha esha bhavatam-antyah pranam-anjalih |
Yushmat-sanga-vashopajata-sukrita-sfara-sfuran-nirmala-
Jnana-apasta-samasta-moha-mahima liye Para-Brahmani || 5 ||

Tapaih santapto gachchhati kila Himadrim
Daridreyena santapto gachchhati dhaninam janam |
Mohena santapto gachchhati vishayan
Sarvatra santapto gachchhati Shivam-eva tu || 6 ||

Yam chintayami satatam mayi sa virakta
Sapy-anyam-ichchhati janam sa jano-anya-saktah |
Asmat-krite cha parishushyati kachid-anya
Dhik tam cha tam cha Madanam cha imam cha mam cha || 7 ||

Dina-yaminyau sayam pratah shishira-vasantau punar-ayatah |
Kalah kridati gachchhaty-ayus-tad-api na munchaty-asha-vayuh || 8 ||

Asha nama nadi manoratha-jala trishna-taranga-akula
Raga-graha-vati vitarka-vihaga dhairya-druma-dhvamsini |
Moha-avarta-sudustarati-gahana prottungga-chinta-tati
Tasyah para-gata vishuddha-manaso nandanti yogishwarah || 9 ||

Jirna eva manorathash-cha hridayam nashtam cha shashthi-jara-
Doshenedam ashesham-eva vapushah sthairyam gatam Keshave |
Kamo jirna-dhanur-yuva Madana-tapo desha-vaideshikah
Dhyanyanam giri-kandareshu vasatam jyotih param dhruyate || 10 ||

|| Iti Shri Bhartrihari-krita Vairagya Shatakam (Pramukh Shlok) Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: भोगों ने हमें भोगा, हमने भोगों को नहीं। तप ने हमें तपाया, हमने तप नहीं किया। काल ने हमें बीता दिया, हमने काल को नहीं। तृष्णा जीर्ण नहीं हुई, हम ही जीर्ण हो गए। भोग में रोग का भय, कुल में पतन का भय, धन में राजा का भय, मान में दैन्य का भय, बल में शत्रु का भय, रूप में वृद्धावस्था का भय — संसार की सब वस्तुएँ भययुक्त हैं, केवल वैराग्य ही अभय है। हे माता पृथ्वी, पिता वायु, मित्र अग्नि, बन्धु जल, भाई आकाश — यह अन्तिम प्रणाम है; तुम्हारे सङ्ग से उत्पन्न निर्मल ज्ञान से सब मोह नष्ट कर परब्रह्म में लीन होता हूँ।
benefits: Cultivates deep dispassion (Vairagya) towards worldly attachments
Awakens discrimination between eternal and transient
Frees the mind from the bondage of desires
Leads the aspirant towards supreme liberation
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Shiva, Vairagya Shatakam (वैराग्यशतकम् (प्रमुख श्लोक)) stands as a sacred prayer. Drawn from Vairagya Shatakam by Bhartrihari (5th Century), this text has been cherished by devotees for centuries. Devotees recite this prayer to lead the aspirant towards supreme liberation and grant fearlessness through non-attachment. It is also said to cultivate deep dispassion (Vairagya) towards worldly attachments. It is especially recommended on Monday (Somvar) and Saturday (Shanivar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Guru Purnima for maximum spiritual benefit. At 10 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 10 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Vairagya Shatakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Vairagya Shatakam by Bhartrihari in Sanskrit with transliteration and Hindi meaning. Key verses on dispassion and spiritual wisdom.
alsoKnownAs: Bhoga Na Bhukta Vayam Eva Bhuktah

seoFaqs.question: What is the Vairagya Shatakam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vairagya Shatakam (वैराग्यशतकम् — Hundred Verses on Renunciation) is the supreme Sanskrit poetic work on dispassion (vairagya) composed by the renowned poet-king-yogi Bhartrihari (c. 5th-7th century CE), forming the third and most spiritually elevated of his celebrated Trishataka — three centuries of one hundred verses each. The other two shatakas are the Niti Shataka (verses on worldly wisdom and ethics) and the Shringara Shataka (verses on love and the limits of sensual pleasure). The Vairagya Shatakam takes the disillusionment hinted at in the Shringara Shataka to its full philosophical conclusion: that all worldly experience — wealth, fame, beauty, sensual pleasure, even learning and yogic powers — ends in suffering, decay and death, and that only complete inner renunciation (vairagya) leads to true peace and liberation. The hundred verses are written in the highest poetic Sanskrit (kavya) using rich metres like shardulavikridita, vasanta-tilaka and shikharini, making them simultaneously sublime literature and direct spiritual teaching. (Source: Bhartrihari, Vairagya Shatakam.)

seoFaqs.question: Who was Bhartrihari and what is his story?
seoFaqs.answer: Bhartrihari (c. 5th-7th century CE) is one of the most enigmatic and celebrated figures in Sanskrit literature — traditionally identified as a king of Ujjain (Avanti) who renounced the throne to become a Nath yogi after the legendary heartbreak with his queen Pingala. The story preserved in Nath Sampradaya tradition recounts: a sage gave Bhartrihari an immortality fruit which he passed to his beloved queen Pingala; she passed it to her secret lover the chief horseman; he passed it to a courtesan; she — recognising the king's love — returned it to Bhartrihari, who realised the futility of all worldly love. He renounced the throne to his half-brother Vikramaditya and became a disciple of Gorakhnath, attaining yogic immortality in the cave at Bhartrihari Gufa near Ujjain. He is also identified by some scholars with the great grammarian Bhartrihari (author of the Vakyapadiya) and with the Buddhist logician — though most traditions distinguish these as different persons. His three shatakas became the most quoted verses in Sanskrit literature. (Source: Nath Sampradaya tradition; classical Sanskrit poet biography.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of the famous verse Bhoga na bhukta vayam eva bhuktah?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 1 of these key verses is the most celebrated opening of the Vairagya Shatakam, encapsulating the entire philosophy in four lines: Bhoga na bhukta vayam eva bhuktah — We did not enjoy the pleasures, the pleasures enjoyed us; tapo na taptam vayam eva taptah — We did not perform tapas, the tapas burned us; kalo na yato vayam eva yatah — Time has not passed, we have passed; trishna na jirna vayam eva jirnah — Desire has not aged, we have aged. Each line uses a powerful Sanskrit reversal (svagata-viparyaya) — the supposed enjoyer, performer, container of time and possessor of desire is revealed to be the helpless object consumed by these very forces. The verse is one of the most quoted shlokas in all Sanskrit literature, recited at the beginning of every Vairagya discourse from Adi Shankaracharya's Bhaja Govindam to modern Vedanta lectures. The reversal teaches the foundational vairagya-insight: we are not the masters of life — life consumes us. (Source: Bhartrihari, Vairagya Shatakam verse 1.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the Asha-Nadi (River of Desire) verse?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 9 of these key verses contains the celebrated Asha-Nadi metaphor: Asha nama nadi manoratha-jala trishna-taranga-akula, raga-graha-vati vitarka-vihaga dhairya-druma-dhvamsini, moha-avarta-su-dustar-ati-gahana prottunga-chinta-tati, tasyah para-gata vishuddha-manaso nandanti yogishvarah — Hope is named a river: filled with the waters of fancy, agitated by the waves of craving, with crocodiles of attachment, birds of doubt, that uproots the trees of patience, with whirlpools of delusion exceedingly difficult to cross, with high banks of anxiety — only the supreme yogis with purified minds, having crossed her, find delight (on the further shore). This single shloka in shardulavikridita metre constructs an entire allegorical landscape of the inner mind as a treacherous river, each element corresponding to a psychological obstacle. It is considered one of the masterpieces of Sanskrit kavya and is quoted in countless Vedantic commentaries. (Source: Bhartrihari, Vairagya Shatakam verse 9 of key verses; verse 30 of full text.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Vairagya Shatakam be recited or contemplated for maximum benefit?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vairagya Shatakam is most powerfully read and contemplated on Maha Shivaratri (the supreme festival of Shiva — the patron deity of renunciates and Nath yogis), Guru Purnima (the festival of the spiritual teacher), Monday (Somvar — the day of Shiva), Saturday (Shanivar — the day of vairagya-tattva, ruled by Shani), all chaturdashi days, every Pradosha, and during Sannyasa-diksha or during major life transitions such as retirement, recovery from serious illness, or the death of a loved one — when the natural vairagya-mood arises spontaneously. The traditional path is to read 4-5 verses daily over a 25-day cycle so that the entire 100 verses are completed once a month. Many Vedanta seekers begin every morning with verse 1 (Bhoga na bhukta) as a reminder before stepping into the world. The text is especially recommended for senior citizens, sannyasis, vanaprasthas and anyone in the second half of life seeking spiritual reorientation. (Source: classical Sanskrit reading tradition; Vedanta sadhaka tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does daily contemplation of the Vairagya Shatakam grant?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition assigns the following blessings to sincere study of the Vairagya Shatakam: (1) Vairagya — the awakening of dispassion towards transient worldly attachments (bhoga, dhana, kama, mana), (2) Viveka — the discrimination between the eternal Atman and the perishing world, recognising that we are consumed by life rather than masters of it, (3) Abhaya — fearlessness, since the verse Sarvam vastu bhayanvitam bhuvi nrinam vairagyam-eva-abhayam (verse 2) declares vairagya alone is fearless in this world of fears, (4) Mental peace — the cessation of restless craving (trishna) which is the root of all suffering, (5) Inspiration to perform real tapas — replacing the false tapas of regret (vayam eva taptah) with chosen austerity, (6) Eligibility for jnana-yoga — since vairagya is one of the two wings (along with viveka) of the Vedantic Sadhana-chatushtaya, (7) Liberation from rebirth — the supreme phala promised to every Vairagya text in the Hindu tradition. (Source: Vedanta sadhaka tradition; Bhartrihari corpus.)
